author: Patrick Stox
title: Google Thinks AI Mode Is Good for Users, but the Content Isn't Good Enough to Rank in Google
content:
Patrick Stox set up tests to see if Google would rank content generated by Google AI Mode in organic search results. Spoiler: it did not rank.

Google believes AI Mode content is good enough to show to users in what will likely be the default search mode in the future. But the content itself doesn't seem good enough to compete in normal Google search results.

The question remains: Is it because it's AI-generated? Is it because there's nothing new added? Is it because it's just not good enough?

Stox was fascinated that the AI Mode content was not competitive with content that Google normally ranks. He was expecting it to rank, since the AI Mode content provides a decent overview of each topic. He didn't expect it to rank at the top, but figured 10th-30th place would be likely.

In one of the tests, a page about a different topic that didn't even mention the target keyword actually ranked much higher than the generated page which was more relevant and useful.

Methodology:
Three test pages were set up. These pages weren't published on a new website; they were published directly on the Ahrefs blog with DR 91 and a history of content around SEO that ranks well. They were given the best conditions possible to succeed.

Two of the pages were about topics the site already had existing blog and glossary pages for. One was a completely new topic where they had no competing pages.

The target keywords were: "hreflang", "technical SEO", and "crawled - currently not indexed". All written content came straight from Google AI Mode responses.

Results:
1. The hreflang page: Never ranked for anything besides some branded terms. Some information in the AI Mode response was incorrect, like the section on preventing duplicate content.

2. The technical SEO page: It never really ranked for anything meaningful.

3. The crawled - currently not indexed page: Never ranked well for anything meaningful. Notably, another Ahrefs page about a different Google Search Console index status ("Discovered - currently not indexed") ranks in 41st position for this topic. The generated test page was much more relevant and had more useful information, but it still didn't rank.

Conclusions:
The generated pages appear fairly useful and should probably rank better than some of the other ranking pages. However, the fact that they don't suggests that Google may have systems in place to prevent this kind of generated content from ranking.

Whether that's AI content detection, something like information gain, or something else remains unclear. It could be multiple systems at work. Stox was running another test to see what happens if human content is added to the mix, after having good results with this approach on a personal test page.
